paper_title: The Last Hundred Years of Land Use History in the Southern Part of Valdai Hills (European Russia): Reconstruction by Pollen and Historical Data

methods.method_name: Pollen and Charcoal Analysis (Sample Processing and Counting)
methods.description: Procedure for preparing peat samples, extracting pollen and charcoal, counting grains and particles, identifying pollen taxa, and determining concentrations for palynological and charcoal analysis.

steps.description: Samples for pollen analysis were prepared using the pollen extraction procedure developed by Moore et al. (1991).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply standard pollen extraction procedure to samples.
outputs: Processed samples ready for chemical treatment

steps.description: The treatment included heating for 10 min in 10% KOH to remove humic material.
steps.searchable_summary: Chemically treat samples with KOH to remove humic substances.
outputs: Samples with humic material removed

steps.description: followed by acetolysis in a water bath for 5 min to dissolve the cellulose.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform acetolysis on samples to dissolve cellulose.
outputs: Samples with cellulose dissolved

steps.description: In order to determine pollen and charcoal concentrations, Lycopodium tablets were added to each sample (Stockmarr, 1971).
steps.searchable_summary: Add known quantity of Lycopodium spores as a marker for concentration calculation.
outputs: Spiked samples

steps.description: A minimum of 500 pollen grains per sample was counted (AP+NAP).
steps.searchable_summary: Count a minimum number of terrestrial pollen grains (arboreal + non-arboreal) per sample.
outputs: Raw pollen counts per taxon per sample

steps.description: Spores were excluded from the pollen sum.
steps.searchable_summary: Exclude spores from the pollen sum for relative frequency calculation.
outputs: Raw pollen counts excluding spores

steps.description: Morphological determinations of pollen were carried out according to Reille (1992), Beug (2004) and the reference pollen collection of the Institute of Geography of the Russian Academy of Science.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify pollen grains to the lowest possible taxonomic level using reference materials.
outputs: Identified pollen taxa list for each sample

steps.description: Microscopic charcoal concentrations were assessed following Clark's point-count methodology (Clark, 1982).
steps.searchable_summary: Assess microscopic charcoal concentration using a point-count method.
outputs: Raw charcoal counts per sample

steps.description: At least 200 items (the sum of charcoal particles and Lycopodium spores) were counted (Finsinger and Tinner, 2005).
steps.searchable_summary: Count a minimum number of charcoal particles and Lycopodium spores per sample for concentration calculation.
outputs: Raw counts for charcoal and Lycopodium

steps.description: Pollen diagrams were constructed using Tilia 2.0.2 and TGView programs (Grimm, 1990).
steps.searchable_summary: Generate pollen diagrams from processed pollen data.
outputs: Pollen diagrams (visualizations)

methods.method_name: Pollen Data Interpretation for Land Use Reconstruction
methods.description: Method for interpreting pollen assemblages, grouping taxa based on ecological/land-use significance, and defining pollen zones to reconstruct past land use history and human impact intensity.

steps.description: The determined herbaceous pollen taxa were grouped into two main categories (Fig. 3). The first group comprises land use indicator plants following Behre (1981), Berglund et al. (1986) and Koff and Punning (2002) that includes pollen indicating arable land (...), ruderal communities (...) and pasture lands and meadows (...). The second group is called NAP (wetlands) and includes pollen produced by local swamp vegetation. At the sampling point, they belong mainly to Apiaceae, Cyperaceae and Ericaceae families.
steps.searchable_summary: Categorize herbaceous pollen taxa based on ecological/land-use groups.
inputs: Identified pollen taxa list per sample
Reference classifications (Behre, Berglund et al., Koff and Punning)
outputs: Categorized pollen taxa list (Land Use Indicators, Wetlands NAP)

steps.description: Group land use indicators into subcategories: arable land (Cerealia, Fagopyrum esculentum, Cannabis, Centaurea cyanus, Convolvulus), ruderal com munities (Chenopodiaceae, Artemisia, Plantago major/media, Polygonum aviculare-type and Rumex acetosellatype pollen) and pasture lands and meadows (Poaceae, Caryophyllaceae, Asteraceae, Cichoriaceae, Fabaceae, Ro sa ceae, Ranunculaceae, Rubiaceae).
steps.searchable_summary: Further group land use indicator taxa into specific land use types.
inputs: Taxa assigned to land use indicator group
outputs: Taxa assigned to arable, ruderal, and pasture/meadow groups

steps.description: According to changes of its amount pollen diagram was divided into 3 pollen assemblage zones corresponding to phases of intensity of human impact.
steps.searchable_summary: Define distinct pollen zones based on significant shifts in the abundance of anthropogenic pollen indicators.
outputs: Defined pollen assemblage zones with depth/age boundaries

steps.description: Interpret pollen zones to correspond to phases of intensity of human impact.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret the defined pollen zones in terms of varying levels of human impact on the landscape.
outputs: Reconstruction of human impact phases over time

methods.method_name: Historical Data Analysis for Land Use Reconstruction
methods.description: Method for reconstructing past and present land use using historical maps, recent land cover data, field observations, and local interviews.

steps.description: The reconstructions of land use at the end of the XIX centuries show that the study area in that time was inhabited and cultivated (Fig. 1а). There are four villages surrounded by arable lands and meadows; these covered 5% and 4% of the area, respectively. However, the human impact was relatively low. Forests occupied about 82% of the territory. The Staroselsky peat bog is shown as a forest in the map of the General land survey. Obviously, the mire areas were covered by pine woodland.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract and quantify land use information from historical maps.
inputs: Historical maps (e.g., map of the General land survey)
outputs: Reconstructed land use patterns/categories for a past period
Quantified land cover proportions for a past period

steps.description: Analysis of the recent map of modern land cover demonstrates that the forest area is increased to 89% and peatlands cover 13% (Fig. 1b). Villages are abandoned, and roads disappear. The arable lands transformed to meadows with shrubs (5%).
steps.searchable_summary: Extract and quantify current land use information from recent land cover maps.
inputs: Recent map of modern land cover
outputs: Current land use patterns/categories
Quantified current land cover proportions

steps.description: During our field studies, we visited the places of former settlements and did not find any signs of dwellings or gardens with the exception of the village of Staroselie where one partly destroyed house still existed.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform field visits to observe current landscape conditions at locations of historical interest.
outputs: Field observations (notes, photos, etc.) on current land cover/features

steps.description: According to interviews with the local people, arable lands were cultivated until 1980s, but the total decline of the economy and agriculture took place after 1990s.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect qualitative data on recent land use history through interviews.
outputs: Interview data (transcripts, notes) on land use history

steps.description: Integrate historical map data, recent map data, field observations, and interview data to reconstruct land use history.
steps.searchable_summary: Synthesize data from multiple sources (maps, field, interviews) to create a comprehensive land use history reconstruction.
outputs: Integrated land use history reconstruction
